title: Pine letter rack
subtitle: A simple two-partition letter rack in pine, screwed and glued
excerpt: A free-standing letter rack from 12 mm pine with two compartments for outgoing and incoming post. The front, back, and two dividers are butt-jointed with screws and PVA glue on a base. The whole piece can be assembled in an afternoon with a tenon saw and a cordless drill.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the letter rack form; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the hall correspondence rack.

glossaryTerms.definition: Timber dried to equilibrium moisture content. Dimensionally stable; does not move significantly after assembly.
The simplest joint: two pieces of wood meet end-to-face or end-to-end, held by glue and screws. Less strong than a dado or mortise joint but adequate for light items like a letter rack.
A small guide hole bored before a screw to prevent the wood splitting and to guide the screw accurately.
The direction of wood fibres. On flat panels, always sand along the grain; across-grain scratches are visible after finishing.

content.text: A letter rack is a good first joinery project because all the joints are
pine 12 mm thick saws cleanly with a tenon saw and takes a screw without splitting when a
is bored first. The finished rack is 240 mm wide, 120 mm tall at the back, and 80 mm deep.

pine: one back panel at 240 mm by 120 mm; one front panel at 240 mm by 60 mm; one base at 240 mm by 80 mm; two end pieces at 80 mm by 100 mm; one internal divider at 80 mm by 80 mm. The back is taller than the front to give the compartments a scooped opening.

Mark each part on the pine board with the try square and marking gauge. Saw to the waste side of the line; pine saws cleanly with a fine-toothed tenon saw if the saw is kept vertical and the stroke is long and light. Check each sawn end for square. An end that is out of square by more than 1 mm should be trimmed with a block plane.

through the base at the positions for the end pieces, divider, front, and back. Use a 2.5 mm drill bit for the pilot hole; countersink each one. Apply PVA glue to the end-grain faces of each part before screwing. Assemble in order: end pieces to base, then divider to base, then front and back to the assembled frame. Wipe away glue squeeze-out with a damp cloth before it sets.
Sand all external faces with 320-grit along the

direction. Sand the sawn top edges of the front and back panels to smooth the slightly rough crosscut surface. Apply one coat of Danish oil; wipe off after ten minutes. Allow four hours to dry. The screw heads can be concealed with wooden plugs cut from a dowel rod and glued in flush.
Wall-mounted version: add a keyhole slot on the back panel and mount on two wall screws.
Three compartments: add a second divider at 80 mm spacing for a three-partition rack.
Oak version: oak 12 mm board produces a heavier, more durable rack that takes a dark stain or oil finish very well.
